violations.rule: Back brevity / atomicity — no 'and' joining two facts
violations.detail: Back contains two distinct facts joined together: 'Stack: the compiler automatically. Heap: the programmer manually (or garbage collector).' This is two separate pieces of knowledge crammed into one answer. Should be split into two cards.

violations.rule: No enumerations, even disguised as cloze
violations.detail: The cloze has two fills (pushed onto / popped off) that are independent facts about function call and return. This is a 2-item cloze enumeration — knowing one (push) trivially gives the other (pop). Should be a single card or restructured.

Card 1 asks why stack is faster than heap (answer: contiguous, no fragmentation). Card 3 asks why heap is slower than stack (answer: fragmentation requires searching). These are the same fact from two angles — knowing one trivially answers the other. Drop one or merge.

violations.rule: Cloze difficulty check — blank guessable from context
violations.detail: 'Stack variables have {{c1::deterministic}} lifetimes because they are tied to their enclosing scope.' The surrounding context ('tied to their enclosing scope') strongly hints at 'deterministic'. A student could guess this without specific knowledge.

violations.rule: Specific constant values without understanding why
violations.detail: 'What is the typical fixed size limit of the stack?' → '1–8 MB' is a specific constant value card. The card doesn't test WHY the stack is limited or what the implications are — it just asks for a number range that is easily looked up and not practically useful to memorize.

The push/pop behavior of functions on the stack is trivially deducible from understanding stack data structure semantics (LIFO), which card 6 already implies by discussing scope-tied lifetimes. This card adds little beyond what's already covered.

Type distribution is approximately: 40% why/how (cards 1,3,4,5,7), 30% definitional/factual (cards 6,8,9,10), 20% application (card 7), 10% other. However, cards 1 and 3 are mirrors testing the same why, so effective why/how is lower. The set leans too heavily on definitions and factual recall once mirrors are removed.

violations.rule: Back brevity — aim for under 10 words
violations.detail: Back is 'Heap memory is not tied to function scope — it persists until explicitly freed.' This uses a dash to add a second fact (persistence until freed). The dash-elaboration pattern is explicitly called out as a violation. Split or trim.

Back is 'Exceeding the fixed stack size, usually from deep recursion.' The comma-separated elaboration 'usually from deep recursion' adds a second fact (common cause). Should be a separate card or trimmed.
